item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's nice to see a real, but obscure object being incorporated into an SCP, and the purpose of it and the history of the anomalous copy work well. However, the effects are a snooze to read. The extremeness of the effects don't mesh with the benign purpose. It also felt like there was to much real-world information description, but I could be wrong, since I googled the article's name and ended up reading most of that info in Wikipedia before reading the article.

One great thing: It's an object what makes you crazy, but there's a very feasible way out of the effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With respect to the list of eight evil thoughts: you have listed "Envy", instead of "Sorrow". Envy was only included as a cardinal vice when Pope Gregory I created the "Seven Deadly Sins" list, a couple of centuries later. Evagrius' original list had sorrow/despair instead of envy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not interesting. It seems more like an article to promote Christian knowledge than an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too similar to the intravenous sins. I also don't like the concept of the early Christian Church being a Foundation-like GoI. It is well written though.
No vote.

Upvoted, but not because of the object. Maybe my ability to enjoy variations on a theme is greater than most, but the "book what makes you crazy" didn't bother me. What pushed it into positive territory, though, is the scope. I'm a lore nerd who loves history screws, and "Oh, by the way the early Christian Church was a GoI." is fascinating to me. It's a space that I will certainly explore if I ever write an article of my own.

Tl;dr- Always read to the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cliché. Downvoted. Also don't read like a proper containment procedure manual.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a CC BY-SA image from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Museum_of_Mystras,_manuscript_codex_4_1642.JPG.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
standard Level 2 (Moderate Threat), Type 3 (Visual Stimuli), Mind-Altering Protocols

I think this kind of classifications are no longer used, so they stand out (in the wrong way). And even as the historical data is interesting, it's also uncalled for and totally not necessary. If you want to keep it, it's fine by me, but shorten it at least.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4th century illuminated manuscript, written in Medieval Greek

There is no "Medieval" Greek in the 300s AD. Minor nitpick, apparently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implications behind this, even if the execution with regards to the seven deadly sins was so-so. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not all disciplines write with the same tone when producing scholarly documents, even in SCP articles. A biologist, a historian, and a mathematician will all write differently. (I've read enough scholarly journals in enough different fields to know.) SCP articles, in my headcanon at least, are produced by the researchers assigned to the project, which depends on the field. This article would not have been produced by a chemist (the sort of person working with an MSDS), but rather by historians and behavioral specialists. Maybe it's just my bias as someone with a degree in political science, but my experience as a scholar (as well as my father's experience as a university physics professor) has been that social scientists (and those studying the humanities, such as history or religion) tend to write more elegantly in their scholarly work than natural scientists. This is of course not to say natural scientists cannot also be good writers - this wiki has plenty of good writers who are also natural scientists by education and profession - but the education of those studying the social sciences or humanities tends to devote more time to learning how to be a good writer than the education of those studying the natural sciences.

TL;DR: Get over it. "Too many words" is not a critique I accept as being valid for any document ever, mine or otherwise. When someone states that something was too long for them to read, I interpret that as their saying that they were too lazy to bother, and I have little time for someone entertaining the vice of sloth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too many words, WAY too many adjectives. Also, it just doesn't read like a technical document. SCP articles are basically Material Safety Data sheets for things that are novel, highly anomalous and incompletely understood. This article reads like an essay, or maybe a research paper of some kind.

item.title: Re: Major Update
item.content:encoded: I actually just had to rename the picture (forgot to change that before posting the update announcement) - try refreshing and see if that fixes it.

item.title: Re: Major Update
item.content:encoded: Picture's borked, but I kinda like it.

item.title: Major Update
item.content:encoded:
I've written an overhaul of this SCP. I'm hoping it is stronger. (Also, as a note, the supplement page no longer exists.)

Couple fun commentary points:

  • This is Euclid, for two reasons: first, the mechanism for how it is not well understood; second, it requires maintenance (rare book care), meaning it can't simply be tossed in a locked box and forgotten about if the Foundation wants to keep it from potentially falling apart from age.
  • In the year 1215, the Fourth Council of the Lateran made it canon law that every Catholic Christian goes to confession in his parish at least once a year.
  • Except for where the documented history in the article refers to SCP-1215 (which is of course fictional), the history is as accurate as I could make it. Evagrius is rather a cool fellow.
  • The bit about crying in the containment procedures is informed from this factoid from Wikipedia: "Evagrius taught that tears were the utmost sign of true repentance and that weeping, even for days at a time, opened one up to God."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like this, but i don't like the scale of it. yet another thing what makes you crazy if you look at it? it's a book. it should only work if you read it, or at least stare at the illustrations. the "no pictures, or else" thing also feels out of proportion.

further, it's clearly safe - as raikonos said above, if it stays in the box, nobody gets hurt.

the interesting part is the 4th century morality, why it was made, and possible cures. making it un-interactable makes it forgettable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this Euclid, exactly? It seems like the Foundation doesn't have any trouble containing it. It would pass the locked box test. In fact, unless I'm misreading the containment procedures, they're literally keeping it in a box!

At the center of the room, SCP-1215 must be maintained in a hermetically-sealed chamber of inert gas with extremely low humidity at a temperature of ten (10) degrees Celsius. SCP-1215 should not be exposed to light for longer than absolutely necessary. Failure to comply with these measures will result in significant damage.

Actually that reminds me of a couple things. First, I feel like the inert gas should be specified. And second, I assume that the "significant damage" would be to the manuscript, but the sentence seems a bit clunky. Appending "to the item" to the end would fix that, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You misunderstand. I meant for no good or interesting reason, which after reading your entire post, I'd amend to 'no good or interesting reason that is actually present in the article'.

As to the effects being obvious

By "obvious" I didn't mean "easy to see", I meant "an obvious choice on the part of the writer, and therefore a boring choice".

Since very few people in the 4th century were literate, making the manuscript affect people who merely looked at it would allow it to help more people than if it were simply limited to those who could actually read written words.

Okay now THAT is interesting.

I also am trying to indicate that there may be a "cure" for the effects, possibly by overcoming the temptations, but that for whatever reason the Foundation research teams haven't figured it out yet.

…Well why the hell not?!

4th century anomalous morality tool sounds really interesting. I understand wanting to stick by 'less is more', but in this case all the interesting elements are in the 'more'. Maybe it's just me, but I think you should put those things into the article, and assuming I liked the execution I'd happily upvote.

To be clear, if the effects were interesting in and of themselves, I wouldn't be recommending that. But they aren't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, Evagrius did write the real-world Peri Logismon as a guide for understanding and learning to overcome temptation (as indicated in the first footnote), so I disagree with that it is "For No Reason". I'm trying to suggest, without out-right stating, that Evagrius intended 1215 as a tool for readers to overcome their temptations so that they could live in accordance with God's wishes. Since very few people in the 4th century were literate, making the manuscript affect people who merely looked at it would allow it to help more people than if it were simply limited to those who could actually read written words.

As to the effects being obvious, while the Phase III effects are extreme and obvious, the time scale and the description of what occurred in the incident report were attempts at demonstrating how the effects are subtle to begin with and only slowly escalate and spiral out of control.

I also am trying to indicate that there may be a "cure" for the effects, possibly by overcoming the temptations, but that for whatever reason the Foundation research teams haven't figured it out yet.

So, I had put some method behind the madness, but I was trying to avoid putting it into the text of the SCP itself - less being more, and all that - but I'm open to suggestions on how to add these to the article without de-mystifying it too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on the current draft, since I missed the earlier ones - I seriously dislike those effects. They're too goddamn obvious. Basically this SCP is a more specific form of Thing What Makes You Go Crazy For No Reason, and it does so in a way I find completely uninteresting. The rest of the article I like. But to me, the lynchpin of the whole thing is that list of effects, and I am downvoting accordingly.

(I do like the use of alternate terms like Philargyria for Avarice, as a side note.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Research is pending into possible interference with Christian religious ethics by an as-yet-unidentified 4th century counterpart to the Foundation.

"I cannot write about all the villainies of the demons; and I feel ashamed to speak about them at length and in detail, for fear of harming the more simple-minded among my readers." - Evagrius, Peri Logismon 16

I don't get how the text in the footnote suggests any such interference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, addressing FuzzyBoots, I removed the compulsion to look, and adjusted the containment procedures accordingly (no need for a large room when a small room will do).

On the Incident Report, I went back through and un-redacted the researchers' names, which accounted for about 90% of the redaction. I'm hoping it improves it - my logic behind the Incident Report was trying to "show, not tell", something that I was initially concerned this SCP did poorly (it's a common comment I've gotten from people on my writing in the past). I'm hoping that helped.

I changed the picture caption; hopefully this works better. If need be, I can remove the picture entirely - there wasn't a picture to begin with, I added one because I thought it helped. I've been wrong before.

I like the suggestion about the D-class testing. I've put a line about a proposal for exactly that sort of research in (it'd be on hold pending the resumption of research into 1215).

On Footnote 2, I'm inclined to keep it in. When I was doing real-world research into the Peri Logismon and the origins of the seven deadly sins for this, I discovered the quote which is in the footnote. It struck me as just too perfect for the purposes of writing an SCP - it read just as though Evagrius had written in one of his own "[DATA EXPUNGED DUE TO MEMETIC HAZARD]" warnings, albeit in the Medieval Greek prose of a monk as opposed to detached Modern English of a Foundation SCP report. Rather eerie coming across it, actually. So, I decided to put it in.

As I've stated repeatedly before, this is my first SCP and I'm open to any suggestions people have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. You could easily get rid of the incident report and condense it into some line like "After Incident Blah and the loss of 8 researchers, further research has been suspended." As it is, the incident report is too heavily redacted and too… impersonal to get into, and I think it's dragging the main article down.
2. The picture: At no point in the article is there mention of a replica being made, so the picture gave me pause and pulled me out of the article (That and "it must not be photographed", which made me question the presence of the picture at all). I can see 3 ways of fixing it: Get rid of the picture, say it's a photo of a different (non-lethal) copy, or put in a picture of a cover.
3. The line "additional testing would be necessary for a statistically significant sample size". Not that anything about it bothers me, but I just thought of something that would be kind of neat. You need data on the book, you have to throw a D-class in there at regular intervals. Exempt the D-Class from termination, throw them in solitary with a camera. Over time, you would accumulate quite a bit of data. Just a thought.
4. Footnote 2 and the sentence it's attached to also took me out of the article for a moment. A fourth-century counterpart to the Foundation?
5. I agree with FuzzyBoots below. Curiosity and the fact that it's an apparently harmless book are temptation enough.

Aside from 1, these are all minor quibbles and I like the idea of it. Witholding my vote pending changes.

Edit: I derped on grammar for a minute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would suggest removing the compulsion from those who have yet to view it. That's kind of a cheap pop for most SCP items and seems unnecessary here. If you want a compulsion, I think it works better for those who have already viewed it, as they find themselves seeking to better understand themselves. That would be in keeping, too, with the original author's desire to inspire others to self-improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made some significant changes to the description, which I think improves the main SCP text substantially. Anyone have any other thoughts?

item.title: Re: Starting a list
item.content:encoded: Make sure that you have a copy saved elsewhere in case this gets deleted before you can do a rewrite.

item.title: Starting a list
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the input, folks, and I'm starting a list of changes to make. Would a summary of the possible effects within the main article be a solution? Are there other, better ways of handling it? What other suggestions do people have?

I'm not feeling that well today, due in no small part to a splitting headache that makes it hard to think, but I'm definitely up for working to improve this article when I'm feeling better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur with the above. If you have to use a test log to tell us what the thing does, it's not a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, and you have a typo where 458 became 485 — for a minute, I was trying to figure out why one of the researchers ate a pen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to say exactly this but was lazy to type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't figure out what the change in behavior is from the reading the main article alone. What exactly it changes should be part of the description, not left to the incident report. Speaking of, there's references to three other SCPs in the incident report (four if you count the accidental inclusion of SCP-485 in there), all of which made me want to stop reading the report so I could read those instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Individuals exposed to SCP-1215, or images of SCP-1215, have their behavior subtly altered, though these alterations do not appear to manifest themselves until the individual leaves the immediate proximity of SCP-1215. Analysis of personnel under SCP-1215’s influence suggests that the alterations to their behavior is based on their prior personality. After leaving SCP-1215’s immediate surroundings, the alteration of the subject’s behavioral patterns increases proportionally to a combination of the physical distance separating the subject from SCP-1215 and the time since the subject was in the presence of SCP-1215. While returning the subject to within sight of SCP-1215 or an image of SCP-1215 has the effect of temporarily reversing the behavior alterations, no means of permanently reverting exposed individuals to their original behavioral state has yet been determined.

This paragraph has the phrase "SCP-1215" at a frequency too high for my liking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The item itself is interesting, but its effect isn't. I like it, but I bet you can think of something more original than having it enhance personality flaws and cause insanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's got some errors in structure. Also, the wording just isn't gripping me.

I'm going to hold off on voting for this until it's fixed. It's a good article, though.
